#  Individual Income Tax FAQs
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Faq ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq) » [Taxation ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation) » [Individual ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual) » [General ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html)
[Filing](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#Filing)  
[Nonresidents, Military, Partial Residents, Residents with Out-of-State Income](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#Nonresidents)  
[Additional Standard Deductions](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#Deductions)  
[Tax Extensions, Late Filing](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#Tax)  
[Other Questions](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#Other)
* * *
##  Filing 
####  [Am I required to file a Missouri return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse1)
You do not have to file a Missouri return if you are not required to file a federal return. 
If you are required to file a federal return, you may not have to file a Missouri return if: 
  * You are a resident and have less than $1,200 of Missouri adjusted gross income; 
  * You are a nonresident with less than $600 of Missouri income; OR 
  * Your Missouri adjusted gross income is less than the amount of your standard deduction plus your exemption amount. 


**Note** : If you are not required to file a Missouri return, but you received a W-2 form stating you had Missouri tax withheld, you must file a Missouri return to get a refund of your Missouri withholding. If you are not required to file a Missouri return and you do not anticipate an increase in income, you may change your W-4 form to "exempt" so your employer will not withhold Missouri tax. 
####  [When do I need to file my individual income tax return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse2)
The due date for the 2025 Missouri Individual Income Tax Return is April 15, 2026.   
Fiscal year filers must file no later than the 15th day of the fourth month following the close of their taxable year.
####  [What filing options and forms are available to file my income tax return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse3)
**Missouri offers the following filing options:**
  * [Electronic Filing](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/tax-types/income/efile.html) (e-filing of your state and federal return) 
  * [2-D Barcode Filing](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) (Filing your state paper return using a barcode) 
  * [Paper Short Forms](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) - [MO-1040A](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040a&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms)
  * [Paper Long Form](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) - [MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms)


**Remember** : No matter what method you choose, your 2025 income tax return is due April 15, 2026. 
If you choose to file a paper return, you can use our [Tax Form Selector](https://mytax.mo.gov/rptp/portal/home/tax-form-selector) to find out which form is right for you. The form selector walks you through a series of brief questions and recommends the easiest form to file. 
[For Individual Income Tax Forms, click here](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=3). 
####  [How do I request a refund for a decedent?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse5)
In order to claim a refund for a decedent, the following documents are required: 
  * Refund check payable to decedent; 
  * Completed [MO-1310D](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-1310D&category=&year=99), Statement of Person Claiming Refund Due a Deceased Taxpayer; 
  * Death Certificate; and 
  * If requestor is a personal representative, verification of appointment. 


Mail all required documentation to:  
Missouri Department of Revenue  
PO Box 2200  
Jefferson City, MO 65105-2200
####  [Will I receive interest on my refund from my Missouri Individual Income Tax Return or Property Tax Credit Claim?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse6)
Under [Section 143.811 RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.811), refunds issued after 45 days from the date the return or claim for refund was filed will receive interest **if** the interest is one dollar or more. The interest rate used for calculating refund interest can be found [here](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/statutory-interest-rates.html) and is set in accordance with [Section 32.065 RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=32.065). 
##  Nonresidents, Military, Partial Residents, Residents with Out-of-State Income 
####  [I am a resident of Missouri. Are the wages I earn in Missouri for "remote work" subject to Missouri withholding?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse44)
Yes. Any time an employee is performing services in exchange for wages in Missouri, those wages are subject to Missouri withholding. This applies in the case of "remote work" where an employee is located in Missouri, and performs services for the employer on a remote basis. This rule also applies if the service for which the employee is receiving wages is "standing down" (i.e., when the employer instructs the employee not to work, but the employee is still being paid). 
####  [I am a resident of Kansas. I am performing "remote work" at my residence in Kansas for a Missouri-based employer. Am I obligated to withhold Missouri tax from my wages?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse45)
No. If a nonresident employee performs all of his or her services outside of Missouri, the wages paid to that employee are not subject to Missouri withholding. 
####  [I am a nonresident with Missouri source income. Why am I required to include my non-Missouri source income on my return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse7)
If you begin with only Missouri source income, your deductions will be too high. You must begin your Missouri return with your total federal adjusted gross income, even if you have income from a state other than Missouri. Your deductions and exemptions apply to your total income, not just your Missouri source income. 
For more information, refer to the [Resident/Nonresident](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/additional-resources/non-residents.html) and [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) pages. 
####  [I am a nonresident. What form do I need?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse8)
You need [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). 
For more information, refer to the [Resident/Nonresident](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/additional-resources/non-residents.html) and [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) pages. 
####  [If I serve in the United States military, what income is taxable to Missouri?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse9)
Military pay is subject to income tax only by the state that is your home of record. If you entered the military in Missouri, your home of record is presumed to be Missouri (you are presumed to be domiciled in Missouri). 
If you are entering or leaving the armed forces and are in Missouri more than 30 days, your total income (military pay included) is 100 percent taxable to Missouri. 
If you are a Missouri resident and you are stationed in Missouri, your military pay is subject to Missouri tax. If you and your spouse are residents of Missouri and your spouse remains in Missouri while you are out of Missouri on military orders, your total income (military pay included) is taxable to Missouri. 
If your home of record is Missouri, but you and your spouse did not spend more than 30 days in Missouri and you did not maintain permanent living quarters in Missouri, your military pay will not be subject to Missouri tax. You must file a [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) and [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) to verify your military/nonresident tax status. Be sure to include a copy of your federal return. 
If your home of record is not Missouri, but you are stationed in Missouri due to military orders, your military pay is not subject to Missouri income tax. You are not required to file a Missouri individual income tax return. However, please complete the "[No Return Required - Military Online Form](https://mytaxportaluat31.mo.gov/rptp/portal/business/military-noreturn/!ut/p/z1/jc9NC4JAEAbg39LBqzOutki3pYOyhCLiR3sJJVsNdWU1f39iQZeS5jLM8MwLAwJyEH0xN7KYGtUX7TKfBb0QC4nlHzHENHIwWloQc4oYWJCtAH8UQxD_3G8AsR2fgViJ7eLR85FwL4kJspAlTso4QUrfYCODg5CtKl_vsr60XQlCV7dKV9p86GVdT9MwHgw08Kq02SlTqtnAb7xW4wT5R8HQJTne9-18YrsnqwSVSg!!/dz/d5/L2dBISEvZ0FBIS9nQSEh/)" to ensure the Department is aware you are not required to file a Missouri income tax return. If you or your spouse earned Missouri source income of $600 or more (other than military pay), you must file a Missouri income tax return by completing [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) and [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). Be sure to include a copy of your federal return. 
For more information about the taxation of military pay, see [DOR-558, Military Information**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/558.pdf) , or refer to the [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) page. 
####  [How should my income be reported to Missouri if I am a resident of another state and work in Missouri but also travel to various other states?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse10)
You must include all income on your Missouri return. If you perform services partly within and partly out of the state, only the wages you are paid for the services performed within Missouri are subject to Missouri income tax. The Form(s) W-2 issued to you from your employer(s) should indicate the state in which the wages were paid, along with the wages you earned in that state. You must allocate your Missouri source income on [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) and complete [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). You must include a copy of your federal return with your state return. Interstate transportation employees who regularly travel and perform duties in more than one state, may only be taxed by their state of residence. For more information regarding interstate transportation employees, you may download the [state of Missouri Employer's Tax Guide.](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?category=&formName=4282)
For more information, refer to the [Resident/Nonresident](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/additional-resources/non-residents.html) and [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) pages. 
####  [I was a part-year Missouri resident. When completing my income tax return, should I complete Form MO-CR or Form MO-NRI, so I am only taxed on the income I earned in Missouri?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse11)
Taxpayers who were part-year Missouri residents can complete either [Form MO-CR](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-CR&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (Credit for Income Taxes Paid to Other States) or [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (Missouri Income Percentage): 
  * [Form MO-CR](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-CR&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) should be completed if you are a part-year resident filing as a resident. A copy of the other state's return is required. 
  * [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) should be completed if you were a part-year resident filing as a nonresident. A copy of your federal return is required. 


For more information, refer to the [Resident/Nonresident](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/additional-resources/non-residents.html) and [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) pages. 
####  [I am a Missouri resident who earns income in another state and pays income tax to the other state. How must I report my income to Missouri?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse12)
If you are a full-year or part-year Missouri resident and earn income from another state, you must include all income on your [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). You may be entitled to claim a credit against your Missouri tax liability for the income taxes paid to the other state. To determine your allowable credit, complete [Form MO-CR](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-CR&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (Credit for Income Taxes Paid to Other States). You must include a copy of the other state's return. 
If you are a part-year Missouri resident filing as a nonresident, you must include the other state's income on your [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). You may be entitled to reduce your Missouri tax liability. To determine the income tax you must pay to Missouri, complete [Form MO-NRI](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=NRI&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (Missouri Income Percentage) and include a copy of your federal return. 
For more information, refer to the [Resident/Nonresident](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/additional-resources/non-residents.html) and [Military Status](https://dor.mo.gov/military/) pages. 
####  [I am a nonresident of Missouri who is required to file a Missouri income tax return. I pay property taxes to another state. Am I required to report the amounts paid on my Missouri return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse13)
In accordance with [Section 143.121.2 RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.121), for tax years beginning January 1, 2007, if a nonresident reported property taxes paid to another state or political subdivision on their federal Schedule A, they must report the amount of property taxes paid to a state other than Missouri as an addition modification on [Form MO-A](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-a&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms), unless that state or political subdivision allows a subtraction to income for Missouri property taxes. [For more information see the Nonresidents and Residents with Other State Income FAQ.](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/non-resident.html#proptax)
####  [When calculating my Missouri income percentage on Form MO-NRI, should I report on Line S of Part B, (Missouri modifications – additions to federal adjusted gross income from Form MO-1040, Line 2) my nonresident property taxes paid?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse14)
Only report the amount of property taxes which relate to income derived from Missouri source income. If 100 percent of your taxable income was derived from Missouri sources, 100 percent of the nonresident property taxes paid were related to income derived from Missouri, and the full amount paid must be reported on Line S. If a lesser percentage of your income was derived from Missouri, you may exclude from Line S, the amounts paid in nonresident property taxes that were not from Missouri sources. 
####  [I am a resident of Missouri. I own property located in another state, and pay property taxes to that state. Do I need to report my property taxes paid on Form MO-A?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse15)
No, if you are a resident of Missouri, you are not required to report property taxes paid to another state on [Form MO-A](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-a&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms). 
##  Additional Standard Deductions 
####  [Can I claim an additional standard deduction for a net qualified disaster loss on my Missouri return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse40)
Yes. The Missouri standard deduction equals the allowable federal standard deduction. If you had a net qualified disaster loss and elected to increase your federal standard deduction by that amount, your Missouri standard deduction will also increase. For details, see IRS Instructions for Federal Form 1040, Line 12e, Exception 5. 
####  [How do I claim the additional standard deduction for a net qualified disaster loss on my Missouri return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse41)
To claim this increase, you must: 
  * File a paper individual income tax return.
  * Enter the increased amount on the Missouri standard deduction line of Form MO-1040 or MO-1040A, and write “net qualified disaster loss increase” on the dotted line.
  * Attach your federal return, Federal Form 4684, and Federal Schedule A.


##  Tax Extensions, Late Filing 
####  [If I am unable to file my return by the due date, can I get an extension?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse16)
If you receive an extension of time to file your federal income tax return, you will automatically be granted an extension of time to file your Missouri income tax return. Attach a copy of your federal extension (Federal Form 4868 or 2688) with your Missouri income tax return when you file. 
If you expect to owe income tax, file [Form MO-60](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-60&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) with your payment by the original due date of the return. An extension of time to file does not extend the time to pay. A 5 percent addition to tax penalty will apply if the tax is not paid by the original due date, provided your return is filed by the extension due date. 
####  [What are addition to tax and interest charges?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse17)
An addition to tax is imposed for failure to file or failure to pay. An addition to tax is imposed for **failure to file** by the due date at the rate of 5 percent per month, not to exceed 25 percent of the unpaid balance. An addition to tax is imposed for **failure to pay** by the due date at the rate of 5 percent of the unpaid balance. 
Interest is imposed on the portion of your Missouri tax liability that is not received on or before the due date. Simple interest is charged on all delinquent taxes. [Click here to obtain current or previous interest rates.](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/statutory-interest-rates.html)
If you have delinquent tax and want help figuring the amount due, access the [Addition to Tax and Interest Calculator](https://mytax.mo.gov/rptp/portal/home/addition-tax-int-calculator). 
####  [What are my payment options?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse18)
The Missouri Department of Revenue accepts the following types of payments: 
  * **Electronic Bank Draft (E-Check)**. With your routing number, checking/savings account number you can [pay online](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/pay-online.html), or call 888-929-0513. There will be a $0.50 handling fee to use this service. 
  * **Check or Money Order**. Your check or money order (U.S. funds only), payable to the Missouri Department of Revenue, should be mailed to the Department of Revenue, PO Box 385, Jefferson City, MO 65105-0385. By submitting payment by check, you authorize the Department to process the check electronically upon receipt. Do not postdate. The Department may electronically resubmit checks returned for insufficient or uncollected funds. If you mail your payment after your return is filed, attach your payment to [Form MO-1040V**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/MO-1040V_2015.pdf). 
  * **Credit Card**. The department accepts MasterCard, Discover, Visa, and American Express. You can [pay online](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/pay-online.html), or call 888-929-0513. A convenience fee of 2.0% + $0.25 will be charged for each credit or debit card transaction.


####  [I am unable to file and pay the tax owed by the due date. Can I get a pay plan?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse19)
If you are unable to pay the tax owed in full by the due date, you may set up a pay plan using our [Internet Installment Agreement Application](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/payment-options/). For further information, email the Department of Revenue Individual Income Tax office at income@dor.mo.gov
Interest and addition to tax are imposed on the tax not paid by the due date. Any portion of the tax you are able to pay prior to the due date will reduce the addition and interest to tax imposed. 
####  [When is a tax lien filed?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse20)
A lien may be filed in any case in which an assessment of tax, interest, addition to tax or penalty imposed under [Sections 143.005 to 143.998 RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.005), has been made and has become final. The lien may be filed for record in the recorder's office of the county in which the person resides or owns property. The lien attaches to real or personal property or interest in real or personal property owned by the person or acquired by the person after the filing of the certificate of lien. 
##  Other Questions 
####  [At what rate does Missouri tax my income?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse26)
[Section 143.011, RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.011) mandates tax is imposed for every taxable year on the Missouri taxable income of every resident. The tax shall be determined by applying the tax table or the rate provided in section 143.021, which is based upon the following rates: 
If the Missouri taxable income is:  |  The tax is:   
---|---  
$0 - $1,313  |  $0   
Over $1,313 but not over $2,626  |  2.00% of excess over $1,313   
Over $2,626 but not over $3,939  |  $26 plus 2.50% of excess over $2,626   
Over $3,939 but not over $5,252  |  $59 plus 3.00% of excess over $3,939   
Over $5,252 but not over $6,565  |  $98 plus 3.50% of excess over $5,252  
Over $6,565 but not over $7,878  |  $144 plus 4.00% of excess over $6,565   
Over $7,878 but not over $9,191  |  $197 plus 4.50% of excess over $7,878   
Over $9,191 |  $256 plus 4.70% of excess over $9,191   
You may calculate your Missouri tax liability using the [Individual Income Tax Calculator](https://mytax.mo.gov/rptp/portal/home/addition-tax-int-calculator). 
####  [Do I need to pay or report my St. Louis City or Kansas City earnings taxes to the Missouri Department of Revenue?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse27)
The cities of St. Louis City and Kansas City levy a city earnings tax. The Missouri Department of Revenue does NOT collect these taxes. You may need to report the amount paid on Form MO-A, itemized deductions. For additional reporting requirements to either city, you may select the appropriate link: 
  * [St. Louis City](http://www.stlouis-mo.gov/)
  * [Kansas City](https://www.kcmo.gov/city-hall/departments/finance/tax-home)


####  [How do I claim gambling losses on my Missouri income tax return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse28)
Gambling income is included in your federal adjusted gross income. If you itemize your deductions on the federal return, (non-professional gambler) losses may be deducted as a miscellaneous itemized deductions. If you elect to itemize deductions on your Missouri return, your gambling losses will reduce your Missouri taxable income 
####  [Do I qualify for a property tax credit?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse29)
To see if you qualify for a credit of up to $750 if you rent or $1,100 if you own your home, [click here for the property tax credit diagram.**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/PTC%20Qualification%20Chart.pdf)
If you are required to file a Missouri income tax return, you may file a [Form MO-1040](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) and [Form MO-PTS](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=mo-pts&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (together) to claim your credit or refund. If you are NOT required to file a Missouri income tax return, you must file a [Form MO-PTC](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=ptc&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms), Property Tax Credit Claim, to claim your credit. 
For more information, you may visit our [Property Tax Credit Claim page](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/tax-types/property-tax-credit/) or email us at  PropertyTaxCredit@dor.mo.gov
####  [Does Missouri have a special form for filing an amended return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse30)
Missouri does not have a special form for amending a tax return. [Form MO-1040 Individual Income Tax - Long Form](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1040&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) should be completed (for the year being amended) with the corrected figures. The box indicating an amended return on page 1 should be marked in the top left corner. 
The long form should be used even if you originally filed electronically, or used a short form. Remember to also complete the "Amended Return" section when completing page 2 of the return. To avoid a delay in processing your amended Missouri return, please include a copy of your amended federal income tax return and any supporting documentation, including [Form MO-A](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=MO-a&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms) (Individual Income Tax Adjustments), if applicable. 
Amended returns currently have the highest error rate among all types of income tax returns filed. The top reasons for taxpayer errors are: 
  1. Entering the wrong amount that was previously paid on the original return. This amount should include tax, interest, addition to tax, and any penalty that was previously paid. 
  2. Entering the wrong amount that was previously refunded on the original return. This amount should include the full amount overpaid, including the refund amount, interest, any amount transferred to next year's taxes, and any amount intercepted and applied to another debt. 
  3. Failing to attach Form MO-A with the amended return. Complete and attach Form MO-A to your amended return if you itemize your deductions, claim modifications, or claim a pension exemption. (Even if changes were not made to this form, Form MO-A should still be attached with your amended return.) 


####  [How do I obtain copies of returns I previously filed?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse31)
The department retains individual income tax returns for four years after they are filed. 
To request a copy of your return, complete and sign [Form 1937](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=1937&category=&year=&searchForms=Search+Forms "form 1937") and submit by:
  * Email: TaxForms@dor.mo.gov;
  * Mail:  
_Missouri Department of Revenue  
PO Box 3022  
Jefferson City, MO 65105-3022;_ or
  * Fax: 573-526-1881.


####  [What identifying information is needed when I write in with a question on my state income tax return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse32)
To expedite your inquiry, the following information should be included in your correspondence: 
  * Your tax identification number or social security number (copy of notice if one was received); 
  * The "tax type" in question; 
  * Your current address; 
  * Day time telephone number; and 
  * Detailed explanation of your inquiry. 


####  [What type of information is needed to support the subtraction for my Qualified Health Insurance Premiums?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse33)
Any of the following information would be acceptable to support your subtraction regarding your health insurance premiums: 
  * A letter from the plan provider verifying the amount of premiums paid 
  * A bank statement showing the amount and to whom the premiums were paid 
  * Copies of cancelled checks verifying the premiums paid 
  * A copy of a SSA-1099 (for Medicare withheld from Social Security) 
  * A copy of a W-2 (for some types of after tax premiums) with a detailed explanation from the taxpayer 


**NOTE: Premiums that are paid on your behalf by your employer, or that are otherwise excluded from your taxable income do not qualify for the subtraction. 
####  [Can the Missouri Department of Revenue help me with questions about my real estate or personal property tax bill?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse34)
No. The Missouri Department of Revenue does not handle real estate or personal property tax assessments or billing. These responsibilities are handled by your local county government. 
If you have questions about your property tax bill, including assessments, payment options, or due dates, please contact your county assessor or collector’s office directly. You can find contact information for your county office by visiting the [Missouri State Tax Commission’s directory](https://stc.mo.gov/directories/). 
####  [Can I pay my tax obligations with gold or silver?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/general.html#collapse35)
The Department of Revenue does not accept physical gold, silver, or privately minted precious metal coins for payment. An exception is for coins officially minted as currency by the U.S. federal government. Pursuant to Missouri statute (Section 408.010.2, RSMo), if you attempt to pay with an electronic representation of gold or silver through a custody agent or other entity, the attempted payment must be converted to U.S. dollars by the custody agent or other entity that received the payment request before being sent to the Department.
If you still have questions, please visit other [Individual Income Tax FAQs](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/). 
Can't find an answer to your question(s)? Email us! We look forward to hearing from you!

#  Long-term Dignity Savings Account Deduction FAQs
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Faq ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq) » [Taxation ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation) » [Individual ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual) » [Long Term Dignity Savings Account ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html)
Pursuant to [Section 143.1160](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.1160), RSMo, the long-term dignity savings account deduction was terminated on September 1, 2025, and cannot be claimed on individual income tax returns postmarked on or after September 1, 2025.
####  [ What is the long-term dignity care savings account deduction? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse1)
This program is designed to allow individual taxpayers to establish and contribute to a long-term dignity savings account to help fund expenses they may incur in the future as a result of being in a long-term care facility. The contributions to the savings account may then be deducted on the Missouri individual income tax return beginning with tax year 2021.
####  [ How do I establish the long-term dignity savings account? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse2)
The account may be opened with a financial institution and must be designated, in its entirety, as a long-term dignity savings account. The account should be titled in the name of the owner, along with a designation that it is a long-term dignity savings account.
The account holder may designate himself or herself as the qualified beneficiary or designate a different qualified beneficiary no later than April 15th of the following tax year during which the account was established.  
Example: Account established September 2021 beneficiary must be established by April 15, 2022. 
####  [ When can the savings account be started? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse3)
The long-term dignity savings account can be established on or after January 1, 2021.
####  [ How is the deduction claimed? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse4)
The taxpayer shall file an affidavit [Form 5872**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/5872.pdf "Form 5872") with the income tax return to verify the amount of their contributions and provide the Form 1099 issued by the financial institution.
####  [ How much can be deducted? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse5)
The deduction may not exceed the Missouri adjusted gross income and is limited to $4,000 per taxpayer ($8,000, if married filing combined).
####  [ Can a joint account be set up for the long-term dignity savings account deduction? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse6)
Yes, although the account owners must file a married filing combined return.
####  [ Can expenses paid out for long-term care of a chronically ill person be included in the deduction? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse7)
No, the deduction is only for contributions to the long-term dignity care savings account under [section 143.1160, RSMo](https://www.revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.1160&bid=48816&hl= "Section 143.1160, RSMo"), not expenditures from such an account. 
####  [ Is income earned or received as a result of assets in a long-term dignity savings account taxable to Missouri? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse8)
No, the income maintained, accrued, or expended in the long-term dignity savings account is not subject to Missouri state income tax and should be claimed as part of the deduction on the Missouri return.
####  [ What happens if a distribution from the account is not used for an eligible expense or was not held in the account for the minimum length of a year? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/long-term-dignity-savings-account.html#collapse9)
If any deductible contributions are distributed and not used to pay eligible expenses or were withdrawn in less than a year from the time of the first deposit, the amount of the withdrawal shall be added back to the Missouri adjusted gross income of the account holder in the same tax year the withdrawal was performed. Unqualified withdrawals are also subject to a penalty equal to five or ten percent of the amount of the withdrawal.
If you still have questions, please check out other [Individual Income Tax FAQs](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual).

Mike Kehoe](http://governor.mo.gov/ "Governor Mike Kehoe Official Website")

#  Request for Tax Return Notices FAQs
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Faq ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq) » [Taxation ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation) » [Individual ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual) » [Request Return Notices ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html)
####  [Why did I receive a “Request for Tax Return” notice?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse1)
You received this notice because the Internal Revenue Service indicated your federal return was filed with a Missouri address, or you had income taxable to Missouri. 
OR 
You received this notice because you are a state employee, attorney, member of the General Assembly, Judicial Branch, statewide elected official, or maintain a professional license in Missouri. For more information, please visit the [State Employees and Professional Licensees – Individual Income Tax Compliance](https://dor.mo.gov/taxation/individual/tax-types/income/hb600.html) page. 
####  [If I receive a “Request for Tax Return” notice, what information should I submit?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse2)
If you were required to file a Missouri income tax return for the requested tax year, please submit your Missouri income tax return, along with supporting documentation. The forms are available on the [Individual Income Tax Forms](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) page. 
If you were not required to file a Missouri income tax return for the requested tax year, please forward a written explanation to the address at the top of your notice. This explanation should indicate why you were not required to file. 
####  [I received a “Request for Tax Return”, but have already filed my return. What do I do?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse3)
Even if you have already filed the Missouri income tax return requested on the notice, please forward a copy of your return, along with supporting documentation, to the address at the top of your notice. 
####  [I was in Missouri under military orders and neither my spouse nor I had any Missouri source income. I received a “Request for Tax Return”. What do I do?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse4)
If you did not earn income other than military income while in Missouri, you may complete our [online “No Return Required” military form](https://mytaxportaluat31.mo.gov/rptp/portal/business/military-noreturn/) for tax years 2007 and forward. For tax years prior to 2007, you must complete Form MO-NRI and submit it to the Department. The form is available on the [Individual Income Tax Forms](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) page. 
####  [I was in Missouri under military orders and I had a part-time job and earned more than $600 of income in Missouri. I received a “Request for Tax Return”. What do I do?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse5)
If you or your spouse earned non-military income while in Missouri, you must file Form MO-1040, along with Form MO-NRI. The nonresident military pay should be subtracted from your federal adjusted gross income by using the “Military (nonresident) Subtraction” on Form MO-A. The form is available on the [Individual Income Tax Forms](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/?formName=&category=7) page. 
####  [I have been stationed at a military base in another state all year and my income is entirely from the military. My spouse is a resident of Missouri, resides with me, and works for a company in the state in which we are stationed. Are we required to file a return?](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/request-return-notices.html#collapse6)
Yes, under the Military Spouse’s Residency Relief Act, if you are the spouse of a military servicemember residing outside of Missouri solely because your spouse is there on military orders, any income you earn is taxable to Missouri. You must complete [Form MO-1040 (long form)**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/MO-1040_Print_Only_2014.pdf). The military servicemember may calculate their Missouri income percentage by completing [Form MO-NRI**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/MO-NRI_2014.pdf). The spouse of the servicemember is not eligible to calculate a Missouri income percentage and should report 100% on Line 27 of MO-1040. 
If you still have questions, please visit other [Individual Income Tax FAQs](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/). 
[Click here for more information regarding the filing requirements of military personnel.](https://dor.mo.gov/military/)

#  Unemployment Tax Relief FAQs
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Faq ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq) » [Taxation ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation) » [Individual ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual) » [Unemployment ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html)
## FAQs for the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021
####  [ Is Missouri following the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 to allow the exclusion of up to $10,200 of unemployment compensation received in 2020? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse1)
Yes, Missouri will follow the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 for the exclusion of a maximum of $10,200 ($20,400 for married couples) of unemployment compensation from their federal adjusted gross income. By allowing this exclusion from the federal adjusted gross income, both the federal government and the state of Missouri are ensuring this portion of unemployment compensation will not be subject to income tax. Only those individuals that had a federal adjusted gross income of less than $150,000 qualify for the exclusion on their federal return. The same income threshold applies on the Missouri return.  

####  [ I have already filed my Missouri return for 2020. What do I need to do to take advantage of this relief? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse2)
If you previously filed your Missouri 2020 return and reported unemployment income that may now be excluded from your federal adjusted gross income, you must file an amended Missouri return to reflect the reduction to your federal adjusted gross income and federal tax deduction, if applicable. Missouri is one of a handful of states that allows you to take a state deduction on what you paid in taxes (less certain credits) to the federal government. If you don’t calculate this new, lower federal tax deduction, you could owe more for your state tax and receive a bill including penalties and interest from us later. 
####  [ Should the exempt unemployment compensation for tax year 2020 be included on my Property Tax Credit Claim? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse3)
Yes, you must include all income received from unemployment compensation on your Property Tax Credit Claim. See instructions below.
  * Form MO-PTS
    * Include the taxable portion of unemployment compensation on MO-PTS, Line 1; since it is included in the Missouri Adjusted Gross Income (MO-PTS, Line 1). 
    * Report the exempt portion of unemployment income, not already included in MO-PTS, Line 1, on MO-PTS, Line 3.


  * Form MO-PTC
    * Report the total unemployment compensation received on MO-PTC, Line 2.


####  [ Since the IRS changed our federal return for us, why do we have to file an amended return with Missouri? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse4)
There are many variables that may affect the Missouri return, and we do not want to presume that there is a blanket solution for everyone.
####  [ How do I arrive at my new federal adjusted gross income and federal tax deduction on my Missouri return? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse5)
To calculate your federal adjusted gross income and federal tax deduction to report on your amended Missouri return, you may need to mock up a federal amended return. This should mirror the federal adjusted gross income and federal tax achieved after the Internal Revenue Service made the adjustments related to unemployment benefits. If you file an amended federal income tax return to claim additional federal credits, you should use that amended federal return in preparation of your amended Missouri return.
####  [What changes will be reported on my amended Missouri return? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse6)
If you qualified for this relief, your federal adjusted gross income will decrease along with your federal tax deduction. If you pay tax in another state or were a part-year resident in Missouri, it may also impact the calculation for your credit for tax paid to another state (Form MO-CR) or your Missouri income percentage (Form MO-NRI). It may also impact the amount of non-refundable tax credits you may claim (Form MO-TC).
####  [ When filing an amended Missouri Return, what amendment reason should I select? ](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual/unemployment.html#collapse7)
On the 2020 Form MO-1040, Page 4, select Box D. Correction other than A, B, or C. Enter the date of your federal adjustments, the postmark date of your federal amended return, or the postmark date of your Missouri amended return.
![Amending Return Reasons](https://dor.mo.gov/img/unemployment.png)
If you still have questions, please check out other [Individual Income Tax FAQs](https://dor.mo.gov/faq/taxation/individual).

#  How Do I Change My Address in Revenue Records?
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [How Do I ](https://dor.mo.gov/how-do-i) » [Change Address Revenue Records ](https://dor.mo.gov/how-do-i/change-address-revenue-records.html)
##  Motor Vehicle or Individual Income Tax Records 
To update your address for Motor Vehicle or Individual Income Tax you may: 
  * Use the [Online Address Change Request](https://mytax.mo.gov/rptp/portal/home/address-change-request); or 
  * Submit a completed [Address Change Request Form (Form 5805)**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/5805.pdf). 


To expedite your address change request, the online service is recommended. 
Additional information regarding change of address can be obtained by contacting the following telephone numbers: 
  * Individual Income Tax: 573-751-3505 
  * Motor Vehicle: 573-526-3669 


## Driver License Records 
To update the mailing address for your Driver License record, you may use the [Change of Address Request for Driver License Records](https://mydmv2.mo.gov/TAP/), or visit any Missouri license office.
Additional information regarding change of address can be obtained by contacting the following telephone number: 
  * Driver License: 573-526-2407 


##  Businesses 
If you are adding or changing a location: 
  * Submit a completed [Registration Change Request (Form 126)**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/126.pdf). 
  * For corporation registration changes, visit the [Secretary of State's website](http://www.sos.mo.gov/business/corporations/).

#  Additional Motor Vehicle Form Information
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Forms ](https://dor.mo.gov/forms) » [Motor Vehicle Form Info ](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/motor-vehicle-form-info.html)
## Bill of Sale or Even-Trade Bill of Sale
This document is to be signed either by the seller alone, or by both seller and purchaser. It describes the vehicle sold or evenly traded (year, make, and vehicle identification number), the date of the sale or trade and the purchase (selling) price or trade value.
## General Affidavit Form (DOR-768)
This document combines six common affidavits in one form. Following are the six different affidavit types. Note that some require notarization of the applicant's signature.
  1. **Non-Use (notary required):** Must be completed when applicant asks to license a vehicle that has not been operated on the highways, and applicant wishes to have registration fee prorated. Submitting an affidavit of non-use does NOT exempt the applicant from applicable renewal penalties. 
  2. **Gift:** Must be completed when a vehicle is given to another individual as a gift.
  3. **Vehicle Out-of-State:** Must be completed and signed by the owner of the vehicle when he/she is renewing the license plate while the vehicle is out-of-state (and therefore, has not been safety-inspected). In addition, the applicant must state that the vehicle has not been in Missouri during the 60 days preceding the application. 
  4. **Abandoned Vehicle on Real Property:** Must be completed when a vehicle was abandoned on real estate that you own or have purchased.
  5. **Repossession Certification (notary required):** Must be completed when repossessing a boat or vessel or outboard motor and using the “Application for Missouri Boat/Vessel or Outboard Motor Title and Registration” ([Form DOR-93**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/forms/93.pdf)). Written consent must be obtained by all owners and/or lienholders on record or notice must be given to them. 
  6. **Other:** May be completed by the applicant when a transaction requires any type of affidavit. In these cases, the form is used to outline the particular circumstances surrounding the transaction.


## Application for Missouri Title & License (DOR-108)
This form has been modified to a single-part form and is now available in fillable format! You must complete all applicable areas of the form. Detailed instructions for completing the application are located on the back of the form.
The Missouri Auto Dealers Association (MADA) will continue to offer a multi-part version of the form which can be obtained online at [www.madastore.com](http://www.madastore.com/) , by calling 573-761-1020, or by written request to: MADA SERVICES CORPORATION, PO Box 1309, Jefferson City, MO 65102. 
## Secure Power of Attorney (Form 5086)
The Department has modified this form to a single-part secure document. Therefore, it is recommended that you retain a copy for your records. **The original secure document must be submitted for processing as copies will not be accepted.** The single-part form can be ordered [online](https://stateofmissouri.wufoo.com/forms/dor-request-for-mail-order-forms/).
The multi-part version of this form will still be available through the Missouri Auto Dealers Association (MADA) at [www.madastore.com](http://www.madastore.com/) while supplies last.
## Reassignment of Ownership by Registered Dealer (Form 2447)
The Department has modified this form to a single-part secure document. Therefore, it is recommended that you retain a copy for your records. **The original secure document must be submitted for processing as copies will not be accepted.** The single-part form can be ordered [online](https://moexperience.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9TDjFUbgTeAHbXU).

# Motor Vehicle and Driver License Office Location Map
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [License Office Locator ](https://dor.mo.gov/license-office-locator) » [Index ](https://dor.mo.gov/license-office-locator/index.html)
  * [License Offices Accepting Phone-In Motor Vehicle Registration Renewals**PDF Document**](https://dor.mo.gov/license-office-locator/documents/License-Offices-Accepting-Phone-In-Vehicle-Registration.pdf "License Offices Accepting Phone-In Motor Vehicle Registration Renewals")
  * [Estimated License Office Contractor Transaction Counts and Processing Fees](https://dor.mo.gov/contact/)
  * [Payment Information for Department of Revenue License Offices](https://dor.mo.gov/motor-vehicle/titling-registration/buying-vehicle.html#pay)


**NOTE:** Video or audio recording is not permitted in areas where other customers are disclosing personal information for purposes of receiving Department of Revenue-related services.
**NOTE:** Every attempt has been made to assure the accuracy of these listings; however, last-minute office transitions may not be reflected in this information. For additional motor vehicle and driver license office information, please see the latest Department of Revenue [news releases](https://dor.mo.gov/news/).
**Driver License Testing** is performed by the Missouri State Highway Patrol (MSHP). [ View MSHP examination station locations, hours, and contact information.](https://apps.mshp.dps.mo.gov/HP32DES/DESMap.jsp)
**Commercial Drivers Skills Testing** is performed by the Missouri State Highway Patrol (MSHP) and is given by appointment only. [View MSHP CDL testing locations and contact information.**PDF Document**](https://apps.mshp.dps.mo.gov/MSHPWeb/PatrolDivisions/DVSD/DE/documents/CDLSkillsTestingInformation.pdf "CDL Testing")
* * *
**Video or audio recording is not permitted in areas where other customers are disclosing personal information for purposes of receiving Department of Revenue related services.**
* * *
Results below are displayed in alphabetical order. To see a list of offices within a certain radius you can utilize the slider on the Text View tab.
By using this map, you are bound to the:  
[**Google Maps/Google Earth Additional Terms of Service**](https://maps.google.com/help/terms_maps.html) (including the [**Google Privacy Policy**](https://www.google.com/intl/ALL/policies/privacy/index.html).)

#  Military Income Deduction Information
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Military ](https://dor.mo.gov/military) » [Income Deduction Information ](https://dor.mo.gov/military/income-deduction-information.html)
[Section 143.174, RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.174) provides a deduction for military income earned as a member of the active duty component of the Armed Forces of the United States. Below is some helpful information regarding the military deduction: 
  * This deduction may be claimed on the Military Income Deduction line of the Missouri Individual Income Tax Return (Form MO-1040). The 2020 return is the first year this deduction may be claimed. 
  * The military income must be included in the federal adjusted gross income and not have been previously taken as a deduction or subtraction. 
  * When submitting your return, include a copy of your Leave and Earnings Statement(s) that validate how long you were on active duty for training or annual training and the amount you earned on active duty for training or on annual training. Failure to attach the required documentation may result in the disallowance of the deduction. A PDF of this documentation should be provided when filing your return electronically. 
  * The military income deduction is available for the amount of pay received while: 
    * Ordered to federal active duty status under 10 USC or 32 USC 
    * Participating in Annual Training (typically 2 weeks a year) 
    * Participating in other active training or schools, except for Inactive Duty for Training, which does NOT qualify for this deduction. 
  * All Missouri returns claiming the military deduction will be compared to income data (Form W-2 data) provided by the Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS). 


[Section 143.175, RSMo](https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=143.175) Provides a deduction for the military income earned as a member of the Inactive duty components of: 
  * National Guard Inactive Duty Training (IDT) 
  * National Guard Annual Training (AT) 
  * Reserve components of the Armed Forces 
  * This deduction may be claimed on the Military Income Deduction line of the Missouri Individual Income Tax Return (Form MO-1040). The 2020 return (Line 18) is the first year this deduction may be claimed. 
  * The military income must be included in the federal adjusted gross income and not have been previously taken as a deduction or subtraction. 
  * The following reflects the percentage of Military reserve or inactive duty income that may be deducted in each applicable tax year: 

Year  |  Percentage   
---|---  
2020  |  20%   
2021  |  40%   
2022  |  60%   
2023  |  80%   
2024  |  100%   
  * Please include copies of all your Leave and Earnings Statement(s) which validate how long you were on inactive duty for training or annual training and the amount you earned on inactive duty for training or on annual training. Failure to attach the requested documentation may result in the disallowance of this deduction. If you have additional questions about this deduction, you may contact the Department’s military liaison at _military@dor.mo.gov_. 


##  Military Income Deduction Update: 
For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2016, Section 143,174, RSMo, allows taxpayers a military deduction for the income received as a member of the active duty component of the Armed Forces of the United States. 
In November 2017, the Department of Revenue expanded the definition of who qualifies for the military income deduction claimed on the Missouri individual income tax return. The previous definition allowed the deduction for the active duty pay received under 10 USC or Section 32 USC 502(f). The new definition includes the amount of pay received while: 
  * Ordered to federal active duty status under 10 USC or 32 USC 
  * Participating in Annual Training (typically 2 weeks a year), or 
  * Participating in other active training or schools 


Taxpayers who qualify under this new expanded definition may file a 2016 Missouri individual income tax return (or amended return) and claim the deduction. In general, refund claims must be filed within three years of the original due date or within two years of the date of payment. 
Forms are available on our web site at [https://dor.mo.gov/forms/](http://www.dor.mo.gov/forms).

#  Motor Vehicle - Additional Help Resource
[Home](https://dor.mo.gov/) » [Motor Vehicle ](https://dor.mo.gov/motor-vehicle) » [Additional Help Resources ](https://dor.mo.gov/motor-vehicle/additional-help-resources.html)
